concepts_tested.source_claim: Neural nets represent a sequence of mappings from raw data to ever more abstracted representations, layer by layer (and vice versa).

concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Renders the same stack of planes but adds moving flow particles, making the sequential, layer-by-layer nature of the mapping observable over time.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Given the depth at that location, recover the 3D world coordinates of the target point, P = z(p) K^{-1} p. As we do not have access to ground truth depth, we will use the function f_1 to estimate depth.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Provides a static grey arrow and text label P=zK^{-1}p, lacking any interactivity to explore depth estimation.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides an interactive depth slider that visually slides the 3D point P along the backprojection ray, brilliantly illustrating the depth estimation process.

concepts_tested.source_claim: We now use the camera rotation and translation matrices to change the camera coordinates to the coordinates defined by the reference frame... P' = MP
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Replicates the textbook's diagrammatic arrow and includes a 'P'=MP' label.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Illustrates the transformation with an animated motion arc labeled 'R, T' and 'P'=MP', seamlessly integrating it into the full 3D layout.

concepts_tested.source_claim: Finally, we project the point P' into the image coordinates of the reference frame p' = K P'.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows a static ray and red point, but incorrectly labels the projection as p'=KP (missing the prime on P).
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Dynamically updates the projection p' as depth or target pixel changes, correctly labeling the operation p'=KP'. It also visualizes the epipolar line, adding excellent context to where p' can land.

mismatches.issue: The label for reference projection is written as 'p'=KP' instead of 'p'=KP'', meaning it projects the target-frame 3D point (P) rather than the reference-frame 3D point (P').

concepts_tested.source_claim: The Gaussian in 2D can be approximated, using separability, as the convolution of two binomial filters, one vertical and another horizontal... e.g. b2,2 = [1,2,1] x [1,2,1]^T
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows a static 3D stem plot of the specific b2,2 filter, accurately matching the matrix values (1, 2, 4).
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Dynamically constructs the 2D binomial filter as the outer product of 1D binomial rows for varying orders (N), displaying the explicit matrix calculation in an overlay.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The filter b2,2 has a DC gain of 16, so we will divide the convolution output by 16 so that the output image has a similar contrast to the input image.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Mentions the DC gain of 16 in tooltips but only plots the unnormalized raw coefficients.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides a 'Normalize' toggle that divides the coefficients by the correct DC gain (2^(2N)), actively demonstrating how normalization scales the values to sum to 1.

concepts_tested.source_claim: The binomial coefficients use the central limit theorem to approximate a Gaussian... Binomial coefficients provide a compact approximation of the Gaussian coefficients.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Only displays a single fixed N=2 filter, limiting the ability to see the progression toward a Gaussian.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Includes a slider to change the filter order from N=1 to 4, allowing the user to visually observe the discrete shape expanding and becoming more bell-like (Gaussian).

concepts_tested.source_claim: The box filter kernel is 1 if -N <= n <= N and -M <= m <= M, and 0 otherwise. Output is the sum within the rectangle around the location.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Displays a static 3x3 kernel (N=1, M=1) matching the margin figure, but offers no way to see how the N and M parameters control the size of the kernel.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides interactive sliders for N and M (from 0 to 2), dynamically updating the bounding support box and adding/removing the blue stems to perfectly visualize the changing discrete domain.

concepts_tested.source_claim: To preserve the mean value of the input, the kernel values should sum to 1. For the box filter, this means dividing the values by (2N+1)(2M+1).
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Mentions normalization in a tooltip ('after normalization the kernel computes a local average'), but does not visually demonstrate or allow toggling of the normalized values.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Features a 'Normalize' checkbox that scales the height of the stems from 1 to 1/((2N+1)(2M+1)), updating the axis labels and tooltips to show the fractional values.

concepts_tested.concept: Velocity as a plane in Fourier domain (Delta Wall)
concepts_tested.source_claim: The FT of a globally moving image is concentrated on a delta wall in frequency space defined by $w_t + v_x w_x + v_y w_y = 0$.
concepts_tested.figure_a_match: Shows a static 2D plane labeled as a 'velocity plane' within a 3D frequency coordinate system, representing one fixed velocity.
concepts_tested.figure_b_match: Provides an interactive 'Motion Angle' slider that dynamically rotates the velocity plane around the temporal frequency axis, directly illustrating how the delta wall's orientation depends on the velocity vector.

mismatches.issue: Introduces 'Gabor' filters and claims the model shows how to pool amplitudes to tune to a velocity, contradicting the text which explicitly states it does not yet present how to estimate velocity.
Introduces 'Gabor' filters and claims 'the brain can decode the velocity', contradicting the text's explicit statement that velocity estimation is saved for later chapters.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 is the superior choice because its dynamic slider and accurate grid proportions effectively demonstrate the textbook's Fourier 'delta wall' concept, whereas Figure 1 offers only basic interactivity and suffers from incorrect grid sizing and severe cropping. Although Figure 1 better preserves the original side-by-side panel layout and avoids extraneous text, Figure 2's functional pedagogical controls and overall structural faithfulness make it significantly more instructive.
